Consolidated Vocabulary – General



ab-dō, ere, -didī, -ditum, hide, conceal. [48]

ab-jiciō, ere, -jēcī, -jectum, throw away. [51]

absum, abesse, āfuī, be absent, be far away, be distant. [39, 63]

ac-cēdō, ere, -cessī, -cessum, draw near, approach. [52]

ac-cidō, ere, -cidī, happen, befall. [50]

ac-cipiō, ere, -cēpī, -ceptum, receive. [49]

aciēs, ēī, f., line of battle, line. [27]

ācriter, adv., fiercely, vigorously (from ācer, ācris, ācre, sharp, eager). [41]

ad-dūcō, ere, -dūxī, -ductum, bring; influence. [26]

ad, to, towards. [5]

adeō, īre, iī, itum, advance; approach, visit. [71]

aditus, ūs, m., approach; means of approach. [71]

administrō, āre, āvī, ātum, manage, attend to. [52]

adorior, īrī, -ortus sum, attack, assault. [62]

adsum, adesse, adfuī, be present, be at hand. [63]

adulēscēns, -entis, m., young man. [63, 64]

adventus, ūs, m., arrival, approach. [23]

aegrē, adv., scarcely, with difficulty (from aeger, gra, grum, sick, weak); in superlative, with the greatest difficulty. [41]

aequus, a, um, fair, right. [20]

aes-tās, -tātis, f., summer. [52]

Āfrica, ae, f., Africa. [80]

ager, agrī, m., land, field, territory. [11]

agg-er, -eris, m., mound. [40]

ag-men, -minis, n., line of march, column. [15]

agō, ere, ēgī, āctum, drive, move forward; do. [68] | agō cum (phrase), treat or confer with. [68]

Albānī, ōrum, m., the Albans, the people of Alba. [23]

aliī... aliī, some... others. [36]

alius, a, ud (-īus), other, another. [36]

alter, era, erum, the other (of two). [36] | alter... alter, the one... the other. [36]

altitū-dō, -dinis, f., height, depth. [22]

altus, a, um, high, deep. [10]

amīcitia, ae, friendship. [8]

amīcus, a, um, friendly. [20]

amīcus, ī, friend. [8]

ā-mittō, ere, -mīsī, -missum, lose. [51]

animadvertō, ere, -vertī, -versum, notice, observe. [61]

animus, spirit, heart. [4]

annus, ī, m., year. [28]

apertus, a, um, open, clear. [67]

appellō, āre, āvī, name, call. [17]

appropinquō, āre, āvī, ātum (with dat.), approach. [59]

apud, prep. w. acc., with, among. [74]

aqua, water. [5]

arbitror, ārī, ātus sum, think, consider. [55]

arma, ōrum, n., (in plural only) arms. [14]

arx, arcis, f., citadel (on the Capitoline hill at Rome). [53]

ascendō, ere, -dī, ascēnsum, climb, ascend. [58]

ascēnsus, ūs, m., ascent. [58]

atque or ac, conj., and; and also. [72]

auc-tor, -tōris, m. (literally increaser), advocate, advisor. [40]

auctōri-tās, -tātis, f., influence, weight. [40]

audācter, adv., boldly. [41]

audāx, -ācis, bold, daring. [29]

audeō, ēre, venture, dare. [21]

audiō (+ acc. and inf.), hear. [12 , 54]

augeō, ēre, auxī, auctum, increase. [39]

aut, or; aut... aut, either... or. [46]

autumnus, ī, m., autumn. [52]

auxilium, ī, n., aid, help; in plural auxiliaries, reinforcements. [14] | auxilium ferō (w. dat.) (phrase), bring (give, lend) aid. [66]

barbarus, ī, m., barbarian. [44]

Belga, Belgian. [2]

bellō, āre, āvī, ātum, make war. [75]

bellum, ī, n., war. [9]

brevis, e, short, brief. [30]

Britannia, Britain. [6]

Britannus, Briton. [2]

cadō, ere, cecidī, cāsum, fall. [50]

Caesar, Caesaris, m. Caesar. [13]

calamitās, -tātis, f., disaster, defeat. [51] | calamitātem accipiō (phrase), suffer defeat. [51]

capiō arma capiō (phrase), take up arms. [49]

capiō, ere, cēpī, captum, take, capture. [49]

captīvus, ī, m., prisoner, captive. [50]

carēna, ae, f., chain, fetter; in plur. freely, prison. [80]

Carthāginienēs, ium, m. pl., the Carthaginians (inhabiting the city of Carthage in North Africa). [80]

castra, ōrum, n., camp. [9]

causa, ae, f., cause, reason. [32]

cēdō, ere, cessī, cessum, give way, retire. [25]

celer, eris, ere, swift, speedy. [35]

celeri-tās, -tātis, f., swiftness, speed. [35]

celeriter, adv., quickly, swiftly, speedily. [41]

centuri-ō, -ōnis, m., centurion (an officer of the Roman army). [44]

certiōrem faciō (+ acc. and inf.), inform. [54]; certiōrem faciō dē (phrase), inform of, literally make (some one) more certain about. [30]

certus, a, um, fixed, certain. [20]

cēterī, ae, a (in plur. only), the others, the rest. [36]

circiter, adverb, about. [28]

circum, prep. w. acc., around, about. [74]

circumdō, dare, -dedī, -datum, surround, enclose. [69]

circum-veniō, īre, -vēnī, -ventum, surround. [26]

cīvis, is, m., f., citizen. [33]

cīvi-tās, -tātis, f., citizenship; state, country. [33]

clām-or, -ōris, m., shout, shouting. [47]

classis, is, f., fleet. [64]

coepī (used in the perfect tenses only), began. [21]

cōgnōscō, ere, cōgnōvī (+ acc. and inf.), learn, find out, ascertain; in perfect tenses, know. [16, 54]

cōgō, ere, coēgī, bring together, collect. [12]; compel, force. [21]

cohor-s, -tis, f., cohort (one-tenth of a legion). [35]

cohortor, ārī, ātus sum, encourage, urge, exhort. [72]

collis, is, m., hill. [35]

collocō, āre, place together, station. [7]

commeātus, ūs, m., supplies, provisions. [65]

com-mittō, ere, -mīsī, send or bring together. [22]

com-moveō, ēre, -mōvī, -mōtum, alarm, dismay. [44]

commūnis, e, common, general, public. [19]

comparō, āre, get together, procure. [7]

com-pellō, ere, -pulī, -pulsum, drive (together). [25]

compleō, ēre, complēvī, fill full, fill. [7]

comportō, āre, bring together, collect. [7]

con-cēdō, ere, -cessī, -ductum, great, yield. [25].

concilium, ī, n., meeting, council. [46]

conditor, -tōris, m., founder. [58]

cōnferō, ferre, contulī, collātum, collect, gather. [66]

cōnficiō, ere, -fēcī, fectum, end, finish, accomplish. [49]

cōnfīdō, ere, -fīsus sum (with dat.), trust. [59]

cōnfīrmō, āre, strengthen (fully), encourage, arouse. [4] | amīcitiam cōnfīrmō (phrase), establish friendship. [8]

con-jiciō, ere, -jēcī, -jectum, hurl, throw. [49]

con-jungō, ere, -jūnxī, -jūnctum, unite, join (transitive). [48]

cōn-sīdō, ere, -sēdī, -sessum, encamp. [39]

cōn-sistō, ere, -stitī, take up position, halt. [26]

cōn-suēscō, ere, -suēvī, -suētum, become accustomed; in perfect, be accustomed. [42]

con-tineō, ēre, uī, -tentum, restrain, hem in, enclose. [29]

cōnor, ārī, ātus sum, try, attempt. [53]

cōnsequor, ī, -secūtus sum, overtake; attain, acquire. [65]

cōnservō, āre, āvī, ātum, keep. [80]

cōnsiliō commūnī cōnsiliō (phrase), by common consent (lit. plan). [70]

cōnsilium ineō (or capiō) (phrase), form (adopt) a plan. [71]

cōnspectus, ūs, m., sight, view. [48]

cōnspiciō, ere, -spexī, -spectum, see, observe. [49]

cōnstit-uō, ere, -uī, determine. [21]

cōnstituō diem cōnstituō (phrase), fix (or appoint) a day. [68]

cōnsuētū-dō, -dinis, f., custom. [42]

cōnsul, cōnsulis, m., consul (the chief magistrate of Rome, two being elected annually). [63]

cōnsulō, ere, -suluī, -sultum, consult; take thought. [68]

contendo ad salūtem contendō (phrase), hasten to a place of safety. [69]

contendō, ere, contendī, hasten. [13]

continēns, -entis, incessant, continual, unbroken, continuous. [42] | continēns terra (or continēns alone as fem. noun, abl. continentī) (phrase), the mainland, the continent. [42]

continenter, adv., continually, continuously. [42]

contineō, ēre, hold together, confine, restrain. [7]

convenio, īre come together, assemble. [12]

convocō, āre, call together, summon. [7]

coorior, īrī, -ortus sum, arise, break out. [62]

cōpia (in singular) plenty, supply, abundance; ( in plural) forces, troops. [3]

cornū, ūs, n., horn; (of an army) wing. [23]

corpus, -oris, n., body. [63, 64]

cōsilium, ī, n., plan design. [16]

cotīdiē (adverb), daily, every day. [21]

cover-tō, ere, -tī, -sum, turn (transitive). [48]

crēber, bra, brum, numerous, frequent. [11]

crēdō, ere, crēdidī, crēditum, believe. [56]

creō, āre, āvī, ātum, elect, choose. [32]

crūdēlis, e, cruel. [80]

cupidus, a, um, desirous, fond (of); eager (for), (with genitive). [73]

cūr, adv., why. [57]

cursus, ūs, m., course. [74]

dē-dūcō, ere, -dūxī, -ductum, lead off, withdraw. [32]

dē-ligō, ere, -lēgī, -lēctum, choose. [37]

dē-pōnō, ere, posuī, -positum, lay down; deposit. [32]

dē-siliō, īre, -siluī, -sultum, leap down. [32]

dē-stringō, ere, -strīnxī, -strīctum, draw, unsheathe. [51]

dē-tineō, ēre, uī, -tentum, detain, delay. [32]

dē, prep. with abl., 1, down from, from; 2, concerning. [32]

decimus, a, um, tenth. [27]

dēditiō, -ōnis, f., surrender. [45]

dēdō, ere, dēdidī, dēditum, surrender (transitive). [45]

dēfēn-sor, -sōris, m., defender. [40]

dēfendō, ere, dēfendī, defend. [14]

dēferō, ferre, -tulī, -lātum, carry down; bring; report. [66]

dējiciō, -ere, -jēcī, -jectum, cast down; dislodge. [74]

dēmōnstrō, āre, āvī, ātum, point out, mention; make mention (of=dē). [44] § dēmōnstrō (+ acc. and inf.), point out, explain, mention. [54]

dēnsus, a, um, thick, dense. [30]

dēsistō, ere, -stitī, -stitum, cease; abandon (with abl.). [74]

dēspērō, āre, āvī, despair; (with dē and abl., = of). [17]

dēsum, deesse, dēfuī, be wanting, be lacking. [63]

dēterreō, ēre, uī, itum, (frighten off) deter, hinder. [32]

dētrahō, ere, -trāxī, -trāctum, remove, strip. [63]

dēvoveō, ēre, -vōvī, -vōtum, devote, sacrifice. [69]

dexter. tra. trum, right. [22]

dīcō, ere, dīxī, dictum, say, tell, speak. [29] § dīcō (+ acc. and inf.), say. [54]

diēs, ēī, m., day. [27]

difficilis, e, difficult, hard. [21]

difficul-tās, -tātis, f., difficulty. [35]

dīligenter, adv., carefully (from dīligēns, -entis, careful). [41]

dīligentia, ae, f., care, diligence. [70]

dīmicō, āre, āvī, ātum, fight, engage. [75]

dīmittō dī-mittō, ere, -mīsī, -missum, send out (in different directions), dispatch, dismiss. [39]

dis-pōnō, ere, -posuī, -positum, place at intervals, post. [39]

discēdō ab with abl. (phrase), leave. [21]

discēdō, ere, discessī, depart, withdraw. [14]

discessus, ūs, m., departure, withdrawal. [23]

distō, āre, be apart. [42]

distrib-uō, ere, -uī, -ūtum, assign, distribute; divide. [39]

dīvidō, ere, -vīsī, -vīsum, divide. [37]

dō, dare, dedī, give. [3]

doceō, ēre, uī, doctum, teach, explain. [61]

du-plex, -plicis, twofold, double. [19]

dubitō, āre, āvī, ātum, hesitate, have doubts. [26]

dūcō, ere, dūxī, lead. [12]

dux, ducis, m., leader, guide. [35]

ē-dūcō, ere, -dūxī, -ductum, lead out. [25]

ē-jiciō, ere, -jēcī, -jectum, throw out. [51]

ē-mittō, ere, -mīsī, -missum, send out. [25]

ē-rumpō, ere, -rūpī, -ruptum, break out, sally out. [45]

efficiō, ere, -fēcī, -fectum, make; construct, build. [64]

ēgredior, ī, ēgressus sum, go forth, leave. [74]

ējicere sē ējicere (phrase), rush out. [51]

eō, adv., thither, there, to that place. [57]

eō, īre, īvī or iī, itum, go, advance. [71]

Ēpīrus, ī, f., Epirus (a district in the north of Greece). [74]

eques-ter, -tris, -tre, of cavalry, cavalry. [19]

eques, equitis, m., horse-soldier; plural, cavalry. [13]

equitātus, ūs, m., cavalry. [23]

equus, horse. [3]

ēruptiō, -ōnis, f., sally, sortie. [45]

esse sunt, they are. [8]

et, and. [2] § et, and; et... et, both... and. [46]

etiam, also; even. [46]

Etrūria, ae, f., Etruria, the district north of Rome. [32]

Etrūscī, ōrum, m., the Etruscans, the people of Etruria. [53]

ex cōnsuētūdine (phrase), according to custom. [42]

ex omnibus partibus (phrase), on all sides. [61]

ex-cēdō, ere, -cessī, -cessum, withdraw. [25]

ex-pellō, ere, -pulī, -pulsum, drive out. [25]

exeō, īre, iī, itum (with ex and abl.), go forth; leave. [71]

exercitus, ūs, m., army. [23]

exīstimō, āre, āvī, ātum, think, consider. [29] § exīstimō (+ acc. and inf.), think. [54]

expediō, īre, īvī, ītum, set free; make ready. [52]

explōrātor, -tōris, m. scout. [16]

explōrō, āre, āvī, ātum, examine, reconnoitre. [39]

expūgnō, āre, storm, take by storm. [9]

exsilium, ī, n., exile. [58]

exspectō, āre, āvī, await. [17]

facilis, e, easy. [21]

faciō, ere, fēcī, factum, do; make, build. [26, 49]

facul-tās, -tātis, f., opportunity. [35]

facultātem dare (phrase), give an opportunity. [75]

Faleriī, ōrum, m., Falerii, a town in Etruria. [48]

Faliscī, ōrum, m. the Faliscans, the people of Falerii. [48]

fāma, ae, f., rumour, report. [66]

fāmam perferō (phrase), bring a report. [66]

ferō, ferre, tulī, lātum, bear, carry; endure, stand. [66]

fidēlis, e, faithful. [30]

fidēs, eī, f. faith, faithfulness, fidelity. [27]

fīlia, daughter. [2]

fīlius, son. [2]

fīnem facere (phrase), make an end of, put an end to (with genitive). [75]

fīnis, is, m. end; in plural, borders, territory. [33]

fīnitimus, a, um, neighbouring, adjacent; nom plur. as substantive, neighbours. [29]

fīō, fierī, factus sum, be made, be done; happen. [71]

fīrmō, āre, strengthen. [6]

fīrmus, a, um, strong. [30]

fleō, ēre, flēvī, flētum, weep, be in tears. [47]

flū-men, -minis, n., river. [15]

fluctus, ūs, m., wave. [61]

flūmen ponte jungō (phrase), build a bridge over a river. [58]

fluō, ere, flūxī, fluxum, flow. [61]

fluvius, ī, river. [8]

fortis, e, brave. [20]

fortiter, adv., bravely, gallantly. [41]

fortitū-dō, -dinis, f., bravery. [22]

fossa, trench, moat. [6]

frangō, ere, frēgī, frāctum, break, shatter, crush. [51]

frāter, -tris, m., brother. [23]

frūmentārius, a, um, of or pertaining to grain. [47]

frūmentī causā (phrase), for the purpose of (getting) corn; to get corn. [32]

frūmentor, ārī, ātus sum, forage. [75]

frūmentum, ī, n., grain, corn. [9]

fuga, flight. [3]

fugiō, ere, fūgī, fugitum, flee, escape. [49]

Gallia, Gaul (the country now called France). [6]

Gallus, Gaul. [2]

genus, -eris, n., kind, sort. [64]

Germānia, Germany. [6]

Germānus, German. [2]

gerō bellum gerō (phrase), wage war. [12]

gerō, ere, gessī, conduct, wage. [12]

gerō, ere, gessī, gestum, in passive, be done, take place. [50]

gladius, sword. [6]

Graecia, ae, f., Greece. [74]

Graecus, a, um, Greek. [74]

Graecus, ī, m., Greek. [80]

gravis, e, heavy, severe, serious. [20]

graviter, adv., severely, seriously. [41]

habeō, ēre, habuī, have. [1]

habitō, āre, dwell. [8]

hasta, spear. [6]

Helvētiī, ōrum, m. plur., the Helvetians (a Gallic tribe dwelling in what is now Switzerland). [69]

hīberna, ōrum, n., (in plural only) winter camp; winter quarters. [14]

hīc, adv., here, in this place. [57]

hiemō, āre, winter, pass the winter. [6]

hiems, hiemis, f., winter. [52]

homō, hominis, m., man. [17]

hōra, ae, f., hour. [27]

hortor, ārī, ātus sum, encourage, urge. [72]

hostis, is, m. (usually in plural), enemy. [33]

hūc, adv., hither, here, to this place. [57]

ibi, adv., there, in that place. [57]

idōneus, a, um, fit, suitable. [20]

impedīmentum, ī, .n. hindrance; in plural baggage. [14]

impediō, īre, hinder, impede. [14]

imperā-tor, -tōris, m., commander (in chief). [40]

imperītus, a, um, inexperienced (in); unacquainted (with), (with genitive). [73]

imperium, ī, n., command, control, rule. [9]

imperō, āre, give orders. [4] | imperō, āre, āvī, ātum, demand, require. [32] | imperō, āre, āvī, ātum (with dat.), command, govern. [59] | imperō, āre, āvī, ātum, order, command (with dat.). [72]

impetrō, āre, āvī, ātum, obtain one's request, obtain. [69]

impetus, ūs, m., attack, onset. [26] | impetum faciō (phrase), make an attack. [26]

in cōnspectum veniō (phrase), come in sight. [48]

in dēditiōnem veniō (phrase), surrender (intransitive). [45]

in eam partem (phrase), in that direction (with verbs of motion). [61]

in fugam conjiciō (phrase), put to flight. [49]

in fugam dō (phrase), put to flight. [37]

in itinere (phrase), on the march. [15]

in omnēs partēs (phrase), in all directions (with verbs of motion). [61]

in statiōne (phrase), on guard, on outpost duty. [45]

in-cendō, ere, -cendī, -cēnsum, burn. [37]

in-colō, ere, -coluī, inhabit; dwell. [44]

īn-struō, ere, -strūxī, -strūctum, draw up, arrange. [26]

in-veniō, īre, -vēnī, -ventum, come upon, find. [39]

incōgnitus, a, um, unknown. [31]

incola, ae, inhabitant. [8]

incolumis, e, safe, unharmed, intact. [19]

incommodum, ī, n., misfortune, loss. [51] | incommodum accipiō (phrase), suffer loss. [51]

incrēdibilis, e, incredible. [64]

ineō, īre, iī, itum, enter; enter upon, begin. [71]

īnferō bellum īnferō (w. dat.) (phrase), make war (on or upon). [66]

īnferō, ferre, intulī, illātum, bring; cause, inflict. [66]

īnfimus (or īmus) mōns, the foot of the mountain (175). [34]

ingēns, -entis, huge, vast. [64]

inimīcus, a, um, unfriendly, hostile. [20]

inīquus, a, um, unfair, unjust. [20]

initā aestāte (phrase) (ablative absolute), at the beginning of summer. [71]

initium, ī, n., beginning. [75] | initium facere (phrase), be the first to, set the example of (with genitive). [75]

injūria, wrongdoing, wrong. [7]

inopia, ae, f., want, scarcity. [16]

īnsequor, ī, -secūtus sum, follow up, pursue. [65]

īnstituō, ere, -uī, -ūtum, undertake, set about; build, establish. [67]

īnsula, island. [5]

integer, gra, grum, fresh. [11]

intellegō, ere, -lēxī, -lēctum, understand, be aware. [56]

inter-ficiō, ere, -fēcī, -rectum, slay, kill, put to death. [49]

interclūdō, ere, -clūsī, -clūsum, cut off. [74]

interim, adv., meanwhile, in the meantime. [47]

intrā, prep. w. acc., within. [74]

inūtilis, e, useless. [31]

ita, adv., this; so. [62]

Ītalia, Italy. [6]

iter, itineris, n., road, march. [15] | iter faciō (phrase), march. [30] | itinere prohibeō (or interclūdō) (phrase), keep from marching, keep from advancing. [74]

jaciō, ere, jēcī, jactum, hurl, throw. [49]

jam, adv., now, by this time, already. [67]

jubeō, ēre, jussī, order. [20]

jungō, ere, jūnxī, jūnctum, join (transitive). [48]

jūstitia, ae, f., justice. [48]

lab-or, -ōris, m., toil, labour. [42]

labōrō, āre, āvī, ātum, toil; struggle, be in distress. [47]

lacus, ūs, m. lake. [23]

lat-us, -eris, n., side, flank. [15]

lātē, adv., widely, extensively. [41]

vulneribus cōnficior (phrase), be exhausted by wounds. [49]

vulnerō, āre, wound. [3]

crūdēlis, e, cruel. [80]

redeō, īre, iī, itum, return. [71]

sub noctem (phrase), at nightfall. [74]

ūsuī sum (phrase), be useful, be of service (literally be for an advantage). [73]

Consolidated Vocabulary – 6 (XXXIX-XLV)


absum, abesse, āfuī, be absent, be far away, be distant. [39]

ācriter, adv., fiercely, vigorously (from ācer, ācris, ācre, sharp, eager). [41]

aegrē, adv., scarcely, with difficulty (from aeger, gra, grum, sick, weak); in superlative, with the greatest difficulty. [41]

agg-er, -eris, m., mound. [40]

auctōri-tās, -tātis, f., influence, weight. [40]

audācter, adv., boldly. [41]

augeō, ēre, auxī, auctum, increase. [39]

barbarus, ī, m., barbarian. [44]

celeriter, adv., quickly, swiftly, speedily. [41]

centuri-ō, -ōnis, m., centurion (an officer of the Roman army). [44]

com-moveō, ēre, -mōvī, -mōtum, alarm, dismay. [44]

cōn-sīdō, ere, -sēdī, -sessum, encamp. [39]

cōnsuētū-dō, -dinis, f., custom. [42]

continenter, adv., continually, continuously. [42]

dēditiō, -ōnis, f., surrender. [45]

dēdō, ere, dēdidī, dēditum, surrender (transitive). [45]

dēfēn-sor, -sōris, m., defender. [40]

dēmōnstrō, āre, āvī, ātum, point out, mention; make mention (of=dē). [44]

dī-mittō, ere, -mīsī, -missum, send out (in different directions), dispatch, dismiss. [39]

dīligenter, adv., carefully (from dīligēns, -entis, careful). [41]

dis-pōnō, ere, -posuī, -positum, place at intervals, post. [39]

distō, āre, be apart. [42]

distrib-uō, ere, -uī, -ūtum, assign, distribute; divide. [39]

ē-rumpō, ere, -rūpī, -ruptum, break out, sally out. [45]

ēruptiō, -ōnis, f., sally, sortie. [45]

ex cōnsuētūdine (phrase), according to custom. [42]

explōrō, āre, āvī, ātum, examine, reconnoitre. [39]

fortiter, adv., bravely, gallantly. [41]

graviter, adv., severely, seriously. [41]

imperā-tor, -tōris, m., commander (in chief). [40]

in dēditiōnem veniō (phrase), surrender (intransitive). [45]

in statiōne (phrase), on guard, on outpost duty. [45]

in-colō, ere, -coluī, inhabit; dwell. [44]

in-veniō, īre, -vēnī, -ventum, come upon, find. [39]

lab-or, -ōris, m., toil, labour. [42]

lātē, adv., widely, extensively. [41]

lēgātiō, -ōnis, f., embassy. [45]

līberē, adv., freely. [41]

longē, adv., far [41]

manus, ūs, f., hand. [43]

mors, mortis, f., death. [43]

on-us, -eris, n., burden, weight. [40]

op-primō, ere, pressī, pressum, press, harass, beset. [44]

oppūgnātiō, -ōnis, f., attack, assault. [45]

passus, ūs, m., step, pace. [40]

per-rumpō, ere, -rūpī, -ruptum, break through. [45]

prae-stō, āre, -stitī, surpass; praestat (with quam), it is preferable, better. [42]

premor, graviter premor (phrase), be hard pressed. [44]

reperiō, īre, repperī, repertum, find, discover. [39]

rumpō, ere, rūpī, ruptum, break, break down. [45]

spatium, ī, n., distance, space. [40]

statiō, -ōnis, f., outpost, guard. [45]

stō, stāre, stetī, statum, stand. [42]

suprā, adv., above. [44]

tēlum, ī, n., weapon, missile. [45]

timeō, ēre, uī, fear, have fears. [39]

facul-tās, -tātis, f., opportunity. [35]

imperō, āre, āvī, ātum, demand, require. [32]

in fugam dō (phrase), put to flight. [37]

prīma lūx, daybreak, dawn. [34]

portō, āre, carry, bear. [4]

praeda, plunder, booty. [4]

prōvincia, province. [3]

pūgna, battle, fighting. [6]

pūgnō, āre, fight. [1]

removeō, ēre, move back, remove, withdraw. [5]

reportō, āre, carry back, bring back. [5]

respondeō, ēre, respondī, reply. [5]

retineō, ēre, hold back, restrain. [5]

revocō, āre, call back, recall. [5]

Rhēnus, ī, Rhine. [8]

rīpa, ae, bank (of a river). [8]

Rōma, Rome. [8]

Rōmānus, Roman. [2]

servus, slave. [2]

silva, wood, forest. [7]

socius, ally, comrade. [4]

spectō, āre, look, face. [5]

sunt, they are. [8]

superō, āre, conquer. [4]

teneō, ēre, hold. [1]

terreō, ēre, frighten, terrify. [3]

vāstō, āre, lay waste. [1]

victōria, victory. [4]

vīcus, ī, village. [8]

vidō, ēre, vīdī, see. [1]

vocō, āre, call. [1]

vulnerō, āre, wound. [3]


1English belongs to a different group of languages from Latin and French, and is more akin to Dutch, German, Danish, and Norse. This group of languages is called Teutonic, and the Teutonic and Latin groups seem to have descended from some still earlier common language. Hence there are resemblances between English and Latin words, even where it is certain that the English word has not come even indirectly from Latin: as e.g. mother and mater, two and duo. Such related words are called cognates; words which have come to us from Latin are called derivatives.

2Y, which rarely occurs, has a sound between u and i (French u or German ü).

3It is properly not a Latin combination, but being borrowed from the Greek to represent a character in the Greek alphabet, it came to be used in a few Latin words in place of an earlier c.

4Some authorities hold that in the case of two or more consonants the division comes before the last consonant, except in the case of a mute followed by a liquid, and would divide thus, mōns-trum, cas-tra.

5So in English contrast tattoo with tatter, soulless with solace, penknife with penny, unknown with unowned, missent with missing.

6Exactly the same thing occurs in the case of such modern foreign names as Paris, Napoleon, Pyrenees; Luther, Berlin, Muchausen; Madrid, Manila, Don Quixote. We give each of these words one pronunciation when we are reading or speaking English, and quite another when we are reading or speaking the foreign language to which it belongs.

7This change in the form of a word to show its grammatical relation is called inflection. Latin is an inflected language, while English is almost wholly an uninflected one.

8It is a rule of Latin that a vowel immediately preceding another vowel or diphthong is short.

9There are in all four conjugations in Latin, that is, four divisions of verbs according to the form of the present stem.

10For the further significance of these endings āre and ēre see Lesson XXI.

11These correspond to the English nominative and objective cases. For similar changes in the form of English words compare the pronouns him, them, whom.

12The verb dō is exceptional, in having short a in the stem, except in the forms dās, dāns (271) and dā (463).

13The English verb face is more commonly used transitively, with the preposition omitted, “faces the province.”

14To the teacher. The translation of the Imperfect by the English simple past tense should not be referred to at this stage; see 109.

15Often by means of may be replaced by the preposition by instead of by with; as to perish by (means of) the sword; to restrain by (means of) force.

16The Latin Ablative case represents three main ideas (at one time represented by three distinct cases): that of from, which gives it the name ablative; that of with, called the instrumental use; and that of in, on, or at, called the locative use. The prepositions ab, cum, and in are used with the ablative to make clearer the particular use intended.

17Accordingly, in translating from Latin, these words have often to be inserted in the English; and until Lesson XLVIII. is reached, are to be regularly disregarded in translating into Latin. At first these words will be put in parentheses, to indicate their omission.

18In Latin, as in English, the present may be used to represent vividly past events or situations. This is known as the Historical Present.

19When a word has -que appended, the accent is shifted to the syllable preceding -que; e.g. Gal´lus but Gallus´que.

20This order (adjective, preposition, noun, is very frequent. Translate as if the order were cum māgnīs cōpiīs.

21Neither the nouns nor the adjectives of the second declension ending in -er are nearly so numerous as those ending in -us.

22Usually reliquus may be best translated by “the rest of”; for example, reliquī Belgae, the remaining Belgians, or better, the rest of the Belgians.

23There are also a few verbs of the third conjugation in -iō. See 287.

24Consonants are classed as mutes, including b, d, g, p, t, c; liquids (r, l), nasals (m, n); and spirants (s, f).

25The adjective is often used with the force of a noun: few=few men.

26The stems of jūs, tempus, and opus really ended in -s, but it was the rule in Latin for s between vowels to become r.

27Latin tends to look upon many actions as proceeding or as viewed from a certain quarter, where in English they would be considered as happening in that quarter; as, ex equō pūgnābat, he was fighting on horseback.

28These phrases all occur in Exercises II and III. These and similar combinations should be given the pupil for practice in declension.

29Translate fugā here by in flight (literally, by means of flight).

30In coepī, oe is a diphthong; in coēgī, o, and e belong to different syllables.

31The Latin pluperfect is equivalent to the English past perfect (or pluperfect), and is used to mark an act as completed or taking place before some point in past time mentioned or implied.

32Where the verb stem ends in a consonant, -tus often becomes -sus or -xus.

33Per with the accusative is frequently used to express the person through whom anything is done, that is, secondary agent. It is so used with both the active and the passive voice.

34The route by which one goes in included under the Ablative of Means.

35With this phrase on is to be translated by in with the accusative.

36The Romans divided the daytime into twelve equal hours; the nighttime into four equal watches.

37Translate ex by of after numerals, paucī, and multī.

38In this phrase certior agrees with the object of the verb in the active voice; but with the subject if the verb is passive.

39After like and unlike, the preposition to is now generally omitted.

40The accusative plural in -īs, as hostīs, nūbīs, is avoided in introductory books.

41Unless some irregularity is specially mentioned, nouns in -is used in this book should be declined like hostis.

42A plural noun may be modified by two (or more) adjectives in the singular.

43Amābar is translated I was being loved, I used to be loved, or I was loved (119).

44Amābor is translated I shall be loved.

45It is not necessary at this stage to commit the whole list to memory.

46Amāverō is translated I shall have loved.

47See vocabulary, 232. The Roman passus is the distance between two successive positions of the same foot, that is, really two of our paces. A thousand such paces would therefore be about 5,000 feet, or, roughly speaking, a mile.

48Compare also explorator, spectator, auditor, monitor, rector, inventor, depositor. Sometimes the suffix -tor is added to nouns, as gladiātor (primarily one who uses the sword) from gladius.

49Dīcō means speak as well as say.

50That is, 'is better than to lay down.'

51The Latin word Scaevola means “the left-handed.”

52Even in the rare cases where either translation might he given, it will be found that the context will determine which is the proper rendering.

53Compare dē pāce, 99.

54Translate ad her by 'until'.

55These phrases, as their literal meaning would indicate, are followed by the genitive case.

56Id is to be understood as the object of nūntiāvī also.

57The participle also, when used as an adjective, has -ī in the ablative singular.

58In the first and second persons, the personal pronouns serve also as the reflexive pronouns; as, Mē dēfendō, I defend myself.

59With suīs, fīnibus is to be understood. English would rather put the noun with the first adjective and leave it to be understood with the second.

60Literally from the city founded; this is the Latin idiom for after the founding of the city. Thus 360 AŪ.C. = 394 B.C. For the ordinal numerals see Appendix (Numerals).

61See footnote 59.

62In such sentences the English idiom omit the conjunction and.

63With tēla conjiciō, translate in by at.

64In sentences like this, the demonstrative agrees in gender, number, and case with the noun. Compare 295, A. 8.

65The ablative absolute phrase as a whole is equivalent to an adverb modifying the predicate.

66For as, its equivalent since may often be substituted. Clauses introduced by if or although may also be used, but much more rarely.

67Notice īdem = is-dem, idem = id-dem, eundem = eum-dem, eandem = eam-dem, eōrundem = eōrundem, eārundem = eārundem

68From dēpōnō, lay aside, because they have laid aside their active forms.

69After proficīscor, in with the accusative is to be translated for.

70Literally upper, that is, northern.

71If for sibi, eīs be read, what different meaning will the sentence have?

72In English, that is frequently omitted after verbs of saying, etc.

73In the future infinitive, esse is very frequently omitted, without any change of meaning.

74The perfect participle of arbitror should be used, as the thought preceded the determination.

75The passive voice has also a future infinitive, which is, however, of such rare occurrence that it is omitted in these Lessons.

76Words like -ne and -que, which are always appended to some other word, and called Enclitics. -Ne must not be used when the sentence contains some other interrogative word, that is, with questions of the first class.

77Such words are etiam, even so; certē, certainly; sānē, to be sure; nōn, no; minimē, by no means.

78This is the regular order when the interrogative modifies a noun governed by a preposition.

79Literally 'other thing,' freely 'else'.

80Translate habeō here by 'hold.'

81Literally 'by means of which.'

82Cōnfīdō is a semi-deponent verb (313).

83The difficulty of the Latin subjunctive is indeed largely due to the fact that ideas regularly expressed by the English indicative require, in Latin, sometimes the indicative and sometimes the subjunctive. That is, Latin makes the distinctions of mood where English not.

84The interrogative may be translated 'which' as well as 'who' or 'what.'

85In indirect questions, as in the accusative and infinitive construction (323), the reflexive pronoun suī in the dependent clause is used to refer to the subject of the main verb.

86For the order of words, see footnote [78].

87Translate 'what'; literally 'what things' (293B.).

88Translate 'in war'; literally 'of war.'

89Occasionally the perfect subjunctive is used to express the English simple past, but this is rare in the best Latin. Of course the perfect and pluperfect subjunctive would be used the translate the English perfect and pluperfect tenses with have and had; but these do not often occur.

90In the former case the dependent clause is an adverbial clause, in the latter a substantival or noun clause.

91The phrase potestātem faciō means give opportunity, give permission.

92This usage is known also as the Descriptive Genitive (or Ablative), and as the Genitive (or Ablative) of Characteristic.

93Trīduī being a compound noun = trium diērum, we have here only an apparent exception to the rule that the genitive or ablative of quality must be a noun with a modifying adjective.

94The height of a wall and the width of a trench are the important dimensions and in Latin these words are often left to be understood.

95In clauses of purpose, only the present and imperfect tenses are used.

96See 386, 123, and 328 for various ways of translating the present infinitive with 'to'.

97Dēferō of those who disclose or voluntarily report something; referō of those who as part of their duty bring back information; perferō with reference to the point reached by the report. All three words are followed by ad with the Acc.

98With īnferō the person on or upon whom something is inflicted is put in the dative case.

99Cum is used also with the indicative in Latin, chiefly in the following cases: (1) to express mere time; as, When Caesar conquered Gaul, it was inhabited by three races; (2) to express time, circumstances, or situation in present or future time (224); (3) when cum has the force of whenever.

In the sense of since or as, cum always takes the subjunctive.

100cōnsulō, when meaning consult, takes the accusative; when meaning take thought (for), the dative.

101The prefix re- has the form red- before vowels, as well as with -dō.

102The object of impetrō can often be best rendered by an adjective modifying the word request; as, hōc impetrō, I obtain this request.

103Use the perfect tense (243 N.B.).

104For the mood see 408, a., somniō being a verb of thinking or perceiving.

105No general rule for the use or omission of cum with the ablative of manner can be given with advantage at this stage of the pupil's progress.

106These expressions have the same meaning, by every means or in every way.

107Latin conceives this as something willed or desired, and accordingly uses the same form of expression as in the ordinary clause of purpose (386).

108The historical present in Latin (59, fn.) may take either primary sequence (according to its form), or secondary sequence (according to its meaning). Compare 427, sentences 2 and 5, or 14 and 15.

109Distinguish clearly between petō with a substative clause of purpose (= ask or request with the infinitive) and quaerō with an indirect question (= ask or inquire followed by an interrogative clause).

110For the subjunctive see 408 a., b.

111The subject is clearly indicated by the subject of the following verb.

112Translate With this design, explained by the following substantive clause of purpose, in apposition with cōnsiliō.

113The distinction between the participle and the gerund, both ending in English in -ing, though with different endings in Latin, should be carefully observed. Contrast the following:

Participle. Gerund.

He saw them fleeing. They are ashamed of fleeing.

Rising early, we set out at once. Rising early will be beneficial.

We lost sight of those crossing the river. We lost much time in crossing the river.


114The nominative of the gerund is replaced in Latin by the present infinitive (124); as, Facile est haec facere, doing this is easy (literally to do this is easy).

115See 432B.

116With intransitive verbs, including those like persuādeō which take the dative case (355), the gerund, not the gerundive construction, is used.

117This will include clauses containing such words as vix, aegrē, and sine.

118Quisque regularly follows the reflexive, as sibi quemque, suum cuique.

119The imperative singular of dīcō, dūcō, and faciō is similarly shortened to dīc, dūc, fac.

120When the subjunctive expresses an exhortation, it is often termed the Hortatory Subjunctive, and when expressing a command the Jussive Subjunctive, from hortor and jubeō respectively.

121Rogō is used like both petō and quaerō (426, fn).

122Translate dējiciō here by deprive.

123Translate the best thing to do; similarly 19.

124Translate by a second (524).

125Since 118 B.C. the southern part of Gaul had been a Roman province. (Hence the modern name Provence.) See map facing page 344.

126Rome is meant.

127Translate by should (or were to). Sī with the imperf. or pluperf. subjunctive may represent future conditions put in indirect discourse (408).

128Translate by came up with.

129Translate by as hostages; for the case see 54.

130See 124.

131To emphasize the connection with the preceding words, Latin often uses quī for hīc or is, placing it even before a conjunction. Here translate by this, literally which.

132Translate by wrongfully (415). For pārērent and vellet see 486, fn.

133See 489, fn. [131].

134Ut in the sense of as takes the indicative.

135Indirect discourse, the verb of saying being understood.

136The ablative, as well as the accusative, is used to express distance. Translate here by at.

137Translate by made the same demands as.

138For the ablative see 492, fn. [136]

139Translate by kept in camp.

140Translate by to remain under arms.

141Itaque here = ita + que; contrast itaque, 487, 1. 5.

142Translate here by among.

143Translate here by among; the phrase modifies a verb of motion.

144Some nouns of the third declension, borrowed from the Greek, have the Greek ending -a in the accusative singular.

145Some nouns of the first declension, borrowed from the Greek, have (in the singular) -ē in the nom., -ēs in the gen., -ēn in the acc., and -ē in the abl.

146Translate autem by now, and frūctū by on, literally by means of.

147After a verb of fearing, nē means lest or that.

148Translate freely by under these circumstances.

149Translate by had heard.

150In double questions an is used with the force of or.

151Translate by they; Ulysses speaks for himself and his comrades.

152From prōficiō, not from proficīsor; note the quantity.

153Translate by as; compare 493, fn, [137].

154Translate as in 491, fn.; although the clause refers, not to manner, but to time (ut = whenever). The pluperfect following should be translated by the English simple past.

155Translate by to one another, literally among themselves.

156The dative, having the force of with reference to, is often used with compound verbs, where it is freely translated by the preposition suggested by the prefix, here under, (sub), and on (in).

157Literally would be, freely would occur.

158See 502, fn., [147].

159Translate feely by how matters stood.

160Translate quī vellet by who was willing or to be willing. A relative clause with the subjunctive is often used to characterize the antecedent as belonging to a class.

161Translate by whereupon, more literally this having been done

162Translate here by at.

163Translate sī minus by otherwise or if not.

164For -ērunt, -ēre also is found, especially in poetry.

165For -ris, -re also is found, especially in the imperfect and future tenses.

166For -ris, -re also is found.
